title: Chapter 2 – Extermination of the Family
content:
The boy squatted and rose, squatted and rose, a massive timber beam balanced across his back. The muscles there stood out in sharp relief. Every few reps he dropped into a deep horse stance and held it.

This was Jeong Yeon-shin, third son of the Jeong family of Henan, fifteen years old this year.

"Young master, the banquet is starting!"

A servant called from the far edge of the training ground. The thick-browed boy did not so much as glance up.

'Let me finish this set.'

Ten more repetitions. Only then did he lower the wooden pillar and snatch up the top he had tossed aside.

His frame was not large. Yet even at rest, the muscles sheathing his body had a density that called to mind cut gemstone.

An extraordinary physique. His body looked nothing like a fifteen-year-old's—strange enough to seem like an entirely different species from ordinary people. Even to himself.

'The Jeong Family Dynamic Technique... it's fully established now.'

His approach to martial arts was nothing like the conventional method of sitting cross-legged in meditation, drawing in the energy of the natural world.

He had discarded the family's mental cultivation technique—all that pointless sitting and breathing.

He took the incantations apart. Reassembled the mental technique into a dynamic form that accumulated energy through movement.

This was possible because he had been able to manipulate energy with ease from a young age, and because the meridians running through his body—the channels where internal energy flowed—possessed an endurance and elasticity on a wholly different level from ordinary humans.

There was a time when he still believed in fraternal affection among his half-brothers. He had gone around urging them to try learning it.

The responses were depressingly predictable:

"You created a martial art?"

"What if someone goes qi-deviant practicing it? Will you take responsibility when they die?"

"You think you're some kind of great master?"

Faced with their earnest mockery and open sneering, he gave up. The boy stopped creating martial arts incantations.

It was a day when first his indifferent father and then his brothers had wounded him. It stung all the more because they were right.

'A great master, huh.'

In the martial arts world, the term "great master" carried a meaning far beyond its common usage. It referred to the founder of a martial arts style worthy of establishing an entire sect.

Setting aside figures like Zhang Sanfeng of the Mysterious Sect and Shaolin's Bodhidharma—who were Great Masters in both spiritual and martial terms—no one in the world of martial arts would deny that the founders of the other nine great sects, who had forged the true lineages of martial study, were anything less than Great Masters.

A grandiose story, to say the least.

Jeong Yeon-shin had never entertained the thought of becoming a great master. He placed no particular significance on having created a martial art.

He just wanted everyone to be healthy together.

So he practiced the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique alone, day after day. Perhaps there was some desire to prove himself, too.

The results spoke for themselves. Every part of his body engaged during swordsmanship and physical training grew markedly stronger.

Internal energy fortified and compressed his muscles. The body he now inhabited was the outcome.

'It's peaked. This is the limit.'

He was certain of it.

The martial art he had loosely named the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique had reached the ceiling of its effectiveness.

From everything he had come to understand through training, his body could not grow any stronger than this.

Short of the ascetic monks of Shaolin, his was likely a physique rarely seen even across all of Henan province.

That is, assuming he was not simply a frog in a well, gazing up at the Henan sky.

"Young master!"

"Coming."

Jeong Yeon-shin answered the servant's frantic call with easy indifference. He understood why they were so anxious.

The head of the Jeong family was a strict, patriarchal man, and Yeon-shin's mother had been the third wife—dead from a difficult childbirth.

It was an era rife with superstition. Out here in the provinces, it was worse than most.

A child who had killed his mother simply by being born. No one in the family regarded him kindly. Not even the family head.

'As long as the food is good, I don't mind.'

Jeong Yeon-shin walked on, thoughts drifting with practiced nonchalance.

With no blood relative who cared for him under this roof, he had naturally turned all of his focus toward martial arts.

He harbored no grand ambitions—no dreams of fame, prestige, or making a name in the martial world. He simply enjoyed the study of martial arts.

As he passed the guest quarters, the sound of disciples reciting texts drifted out.

"Recite Chapter 1, Section 4 of the Great Ming Code."

"The compatriots with long ears and small stature returned from the gates of heaven, destroyed the evil Yuan, and established the great Ming with Taizu."

"Chapter 1, Section 8."

"A giant demon chased after the compatriots, but the gates were sealed in time and it was annihilated."

Familiar passages. Jeong Yeon-shin smiled faintly as he walked past.

"Late again, you fool."

His eldest half-brother's warm greeting awaited him at the banquet hall.

Jeong Yeon-shin's pectoral muscles twitched involuntarily under the weight of those narrow, glaring eyes.

"It's been a while, brother."

"If you're going to be late, at least sit down quickly."

Jeong Nam-san, eldest son of the Jeong family, said with a snort.

Jeong Yeon-shin dipped his head and took stock of the room as he settled into the seat at the far end of the long table.

The family head presided at the head, the first and second wives flanking him, then Jeong Nam-san with his wife and daughter, on down to Jeong Yeon-shin's fifth older sister.

By any reasonable standard, he should not have been seated at the lowest position when younger siblings were present. But the family head's indifference and the constant needling from the first and second wives had long reduced him to a direct descendant in name only.

'Convenient, really.'

No important family duties meant no obligations, no responsibilities. The meals were still good, so he could devote himself entirely to training.

'They must be thrilled.'

The family head's beaming gaze was fixed on the second son—the one with the angular jaw.

This banquet was a farewell dinner. The second son would soon depart to study Taoist martial arts.

The Southern Edge Sect. The largest sect in Shaanxi province, rivaling small nations in scale.

Nowadays it stood among the nine major sects, vying with Mount Hua for the title of Shaanxi's premier faction.

To become a disciple of one of those nine sects was a tremendous honor.

The feast was lavish—remarkably so, given that the continent was rotting from famine.

When the family head raised his cup, every member of the household followed suit.

"Since my late father, a disciple of the Southern Edge Sect, founded this family, I have devoted my life to reviving our clan. Had I wished to make a name for myself in the wider world, I too would have inherited the Taoist techniques! But how could I abandon the family's affairs? Now my son will follow in his grandfather's footsteps and ascend Mount Zhongnan. I could not be more overjoyed."

"A fine day indeed."

"Congratulations, brother."

The family head, claiming he could have joined the sect if only he had wanted to.

The family members, falling over themselves to flatter both him and the second son.

In the martial arts world, it was better to reign as the head of a snake than serve as the tail of a dragon.

The Jeong family was the de facto law enforcement and the largest landowner in the county. A martial arts household whose influence in Xinye County rivaled the magistrate's own.

Around here, they had no reason to envy the major sects.

'Join the Southern Edge as a low-ranking disciple and you'll spend your days dusting the ancestral tablets of your seniors.'

Jeong Yeon-shin ate his fill in silence.

"Wouldn't you say the Southern Edge Sect has recently pulled half a rank above Mount Hua?"

"Brilliant new masters have come down from the mountain."

"Judging by the Shaanxi tournament, that certainly seems to be the case. The Cloud Sword Dragon beheaded the leader of the evil sect forces that erupted in the stands. The Plum Blossom Sword Masters are said to be formidable, but they're probably no match for Senior Brother Sword Dragon."

The second son was already calling the Southern Edge's rising star "senior brother," as though he had been accepted as a disciple.

Jeong Yeon-shin let out a quiet chuckle and reached for a rice cake—then froze.

The family head was watching him with disapproving eyes.

'Should I have joined in the flattery?'

The family head opened his mouth, deliberate and slow.

"I hear the third son spends all day at the training ground. How goes your progress with the Jeong Clan's Divine Sword?"

"The principles of our family's art are difficult for me to grasp, so I have made little headway. I am ashamed."

Jeong Yeon-shin answered with careful politeness, straightening in his seat.

The Jeong Clan's Divine Sword was the family's signature martial art.

As with such things everywhere, the name was grand, but the reality was a sword technique cobbled together from a partial understanding of the first half of the Southern Edge's Thirty-Six Heavenly Swords.

This was how secular disciples of Taoist sects like the Southern Edge and Mount Hua typically established their own styles.

By sect law, it was a grave transgression—grounds for the extermination of the entire clan.

In practice, however, as long as appropriate donations flowed regularly to the main sect, they looked the other way.

The sect received wealth and provisions. The secular branch gained the protection of the sect's name.

A mutually beneficial arrangement.

'The Divine Sword is exactly why I focused on the dynamic technique.'

There was too much wrong with it.

Even at a glance, the sword paths that made no sense were glaringly obvious.

If he ever formally joined, it was certain he would not be able to stop himself from modifying the technique.

There are cases where the wounds of childhood dictate the course of youth.

So it was for Jeong Yeon-shin.

His brothers' mockery had irrationally driven the boy to channel everything into the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique alone.

The family head clicked his tongue.

"Your first and second brothers are already approaching mastery. I commend your diligence and will not press you further, but you should reflect on whether you are truly making the most of your time at the training ground."

"I will take your words to heart."

Jeong Yeon-shin clasped his hands and bowed, holding the posture until the family head's gaze drifted elsewhere. He paid no attention to the looks his siblings were casting his way.

'Drawing attention only brings pain.'

Jeong Yeon-shin turned back to the banquet.

He placed a piece of properly fried chicken in his mouth and let the conversations around him wash over him.

"With the evil sect incident in Guanzhong, the situation is looking grim. Chief Steward."

"Yes, Family Head."

"Are the intelligence networks taking shape? I'm not expecting anything on the level of the Beggar Sect or the Hao Clan. But we should at least be able to keep pace with the major sects' agents, wouldn't you say?"

"We should be able to manage that much, at least within Xinye."

"Good. Good."

That was when it happened.

"Southern Edge masters have arrived!"

The voice belonged to Zhang, the gatekeeper. The banquet hall erupted into chaos.

Word had come that someone would arrive to escort the Jeong family's second son, but no one had expected the timing.

"Quick—welcome them at once! No, I'll go myself!"

The family head, who had been seated with rigid composure only moments before, sprang to his feet and hurried out to receive the honored guests.

Everyone else did the same.

'Now I'm curious.'

Jeong Yeon-shin disliked bothersome affairs. Under normal circumstances, he would have slipped away to the training ground without a word.

But this was one of the nine major sects. Taoist masters were a different matter entirely.

When else would he have the chance to see renowned martial artists from a sect whose name echoed across the world?

"What kind of master do you think it is?"

"Could it be the Cloud Sword Dragon himself? Or perhaps the elders?"

For once, the siblings looked like children, wide-eyed with anticipation.

Jeong Yeon-shin fell in with the crowd and followed them toward the main gate.

The family head, who had sprinted ahead using qinggong, was bowing deeply with clasped hands before three figures.

"I am overwhelmed to receive such esteemed guests at our humble estate. I am Jeong Dae-myeong, head of the family."

The three returned the greeting, but the woman at the front commanded immediate attention.

Hair like molten gold. Long, leaf-shaped ears.

Her features were beautiful and utterly alien, like a celestial being descending from the heavens.

"An Elf...!"

The eldest son, Jeong Nam-san, breathed the word like a groan. A glance back confirmed he was staring shamelessly. Jeong Yeon-shin clicked his tongue inwardly.

'So Elves really do look the way we were taught. Fascinating.'

Xinye County had once been renowned as a hub of trade and transport, but after the landscape was scarred by battles between strange beings at the end of the Yuan dynasty, it had shrunk to a modest village.

Villagers did not often encounter Elves.

The "compatriots of the Central Plains"—said to have descended from the heavens and founded the Ming alongside the first emperor—could only be found in Beijing, in military encampments, at the major sects, or within the most prestigious families.

'An Elf physician came to the house when I was born, they say.'

Beyond that, he knew nothing.

He could not ask the family head, and the servants who might have known kept their distance from Jeong Yeon-shin.

"I am deeply grateful for your warm welcome. My name is Yeo Il-shin, and I serve as an elder of the Southern Edge Sect."

The Elf elder of the Southern Edge smiled. It was a lovely smile, the kind that seemed to wash one's eyes clean—yet no one with any sense of propriety dared smile back.

'An Elf elder of a major sect?'

Then she must be a founding contributor to the Great Ming Empire itself. Elves were said not to age.

She had likely played no small role in driving out the Yuan dynasty alongside the Southern Edge Sect's founding ancestor.

"I—I failed to recognize an immortal!"

It was not just the family head.

The household members behind him, along with onlookers who had emerged from every corner, all clasped their hands and bowed low. Some dropped to their knees entirely.

'Isn't that a bit much?'

Jeong Yeon-shin, who had offered a measured bow with clasped hands, lifted his head slightly.

In that moment, his gaze met Yeo Il-shin's—and she was looking at him with a curious expression.
